title: Microsoft Vows To Appeal EU Ruling
author: Elizabeth Millard, E-Commerce Times
abstract: TechNewsWorld/E-Commerce Times article (Jun 8 2004, Elizabeth Millard) covering Microsoft's Jun 7 2004 filing with the European Court of Justice to annul the EC's Mar 24 2004 antitrust ruling. The ruling required Windows-Media-Player unbundling, interoperability disclosures to competitors, and a €497M ($612M) fine. Aberdeen chief research officer Peter Kastner framed the appeal as a broader sovereignty dispute: Microsoft has 'silent friends' among global firms, and 'for the EU to set a totally different standard for business conduct threatens not only Microsoft, but every other global company as well.' Yankee Group's Laura DiDio notes Microsoft will pay the fine 'at the last minute.' Sun Microsystems was the lead EC complainant; Microsoft's Apr 2004 $2B Sun settlement raised questions about the appeal's strength.
importance_rationale: Documents the opening of Microsoft's EU antitrust appeal — the landmark case establishing EC antitrust jurisdiction over U.S. tech giants that remains the template for EU action against Google, Apple, Meta, and Amazon through 2025.
relevance_rationale: EU-vs-US-Big-Tech tensions over sovereignty-of-regulation are a live 2020s issue (Digital Markets Act, Digital Services Act); Kastner's 'threatens every global company' framing is the exact concern Silicon Valley raised in 2023+.
prescience_rationale: Kastner correctly framed the case as precedent-setting for ALL global companies. Microsoft lost its appeal in Sep 2007 (CFI), dropped further appeal 2012. The EU has since fined Google ($8.25B cumulative), Intel (€1.06B), Apple (€13B tax), Meta, Amazon, and ruled broadly under DMA/DSA 2022-2024. DiDio's 'long shot' appeal prediction was correct. Silent friends did emerge: many US tech firms filed amicus briefs throughout 2000s and again under DMA 2022-2024.
